ontology.foodex2_description: Yellow noodle dish with clear spiced chicken broth (Malay mee soto style)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre bowl of Mee Soto weighs approximately 500g, comprising yellow egg noodles, spiced chicken broth, shredded chicken, bean sprouts, fried shallots, and sambal kicap.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Soto — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mee Soto. This is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Micronutrients copper, manganese, and vitamin K were supplemented from enriched JSON estimates (confidence 0.9) where HPB data was not available.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Yellow Egg Noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Mee Soto as a composite dish. GI of 55 is estimated from yellow egg noodles (GI range 50–57 per University of Sydney GI Database). The clear chicken broth, protein (6.2g/100g), and fat (2.2g/100g) components slow carbohydrate absorption, supporting a low-to-medium GI classification. Glycemic load per 500g serving calculated as: (55 × 61g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 33.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seasoned chicken broth and sambal kicap
hypertension.guidance: A standard 500g bowl of Mee Soto delivers approximately 2,050mg sodium — approximately 85% of the 2,400mg daily recommended limit — so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt broth, consume only half the broth, and avoid adding extra sambal kicap.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~33.6 per 500g bowl) from yellow egg noodles, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mee Soto has a low GI of approximately 55 and a moderate glycemic load of 33.6 per bowl; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, favour a smaller 300–350g serving, and pair with additional protein to further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.5g/100g), zero trans fat, and anti-inflammatory spice profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mee Soto is relatively heart-friendly with low saturated fat (0.5g/100g), zero trans fat, and a spice profile rich in turmeric (curcumin), lemongrass, and galangal that confer anti-inflammatory benefits; the primary cardiovascular concern is sodium, which should be managed by limiting broth consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric, lemongrass, and galangal
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The turmeric (curcumin), lemongrass, and galangal in Mee Soto broth have documented anti-inflammatory properties that may provide modest benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, and the low fat content (2.2g/100g) makes it a suitable warm meal option.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Soto is a popular Malay-Singaporean noodle soup dish consisting of yellow egg noodles served in a clear, aromatic, golden-coloured chicken broth (Soto Ayam style). The broth is traditionally infused with turmeric, lemongrass, galangal, and ginger, giving it a fragrant, mildly spiced flavour that is lighter and more delicate than the thick sweet-potato-based gravy of its cousin Mee Rebus. The dish is topped with shredded poached chicken, blanched bean sprouts, fried shallots, and Chinese celery, and is typically served with sambal kicap (spiced sweet soy sauce) on the side. Mee Soto is one of the lower-calorie options at Malay hawker stalls, providing approximately 92 kcal per 100g and 460 kcal per standard 500g bowl. It delivers 6.2g protein and only 2.2g fat per 100g, with a low glycemic index of approximately 55 due to the moderating effect of protein and fat on carbohydrate absorption from the yellow egg noodles. Sodium is the primary nutritional concern at 410mg per 100g (approximately 2,050mg per bowl), making broth management important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The spice profile — particularly turmeric curcumin — provides anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular health. Mee Soto is halal, dairy-free, and widely available at Malay hawker centres and kopitiams in Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia.
common_user_queries: mee soto vs mee rebus difference

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using yellow egg noodles as the closest analogous food (GI 50–57). Micronutrients copper, manganese, and vitamin K supplemented from enriched JSON estimates at confidence 0.9. Overall record confidence is high given the primary approved source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Mee Soto is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). Copper (0.06mg/100g), manganese (0.1mg/100g), and vitamin K (4.0mcg/100g) values are from enriched JSON estimates (confidence 0.9) as these micronutrients were not individually reported in the HPB Foodo entry. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on yellow egg noodles (GI 50–57, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct composite-dish GI measurement for Mee Soto exists in the published literature. Two default portions were present in the input (500g and 450g); the 500g bowl is adopted as the primary serving size per HPB Foodo convention. Hindi local name मी सोटो is a transliteration added to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirements.
